items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.q: Whose triadic sign distinguishes icon, index and symbol?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Peirce. “Saussure” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Whose 1964 Rhetoric of the Image analysed Panzani's pasta ad?
items.why: Correct answer: Barthes. “Metz” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Mitchell publish Picture Theory?
items.why: The real figure is 1994 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
